summary: Dhikr is the remembrance and invocation of God, the repeated mention of His names or of set formulae, described by Sufi authors as the central practice of the spiritual path. It is grounded in Qur'anic injunctions to remember God, and is performed both individually and communally, the latter often in the assemblies of the Sufi orders.
description:
Dhikr, from the Arabic root meaning to remember, mention or recollect, denotes the remembrance and invocation of God through the repeated utterance of His names, of the testimony of faith (la ilaha illa Allah), or of other set formulae. Sufi authors ground the practice in the Qur'an, which repeatedly enjoins the believers to remember God much and speaks of the remembrance of God as bringing tranquillity to hearts, and in the example (sunna) of the Prophet. In the vocabulary of Sufism (tasawwuf), dhikr is treated as the central discipline of the path: a means of purifying the heart, of maintaining the consciousness of God's presence, and of drawing the invoker toward the Named through the Name.

The manuals and the orders distinguish outward and inward forms, dhikr of the tongue and dhikr of the heart, and individual from communal performance. Individual dhikr is often practised under the guidance of a master (shaykh) following initiation, sometimes with prescribed formulae and numbers. Communal dhikr, performed in the gatherings of the tariqas, may be vocal and rhythmic and is in some orders joined with spiritual audition (sama'), movement or breath control; the specific forms vary widely between orders such as the Qadiriyya, Shadhiliyya, Naqshbandiyya, Chishtiyya and Mevleviyya. Some of these practices, and the assemblies around them, have been objects of criticism from other Muslim scholars, while Sufi authors defend them as rooted in the Qur'an and the prophetic example. This entry reports the practice and its rationale as given by the tradition and does not adjudicate such disputes.
editorial_notes: Scope: dhikr as the central devotional practice of Sufism, its Qur'anic grounding, and its individual and communal forms. Reported descriptively, not in a devotional register, per neutrality principle #4; the Qur'anic grounding and the prophetic example are given as the tradition's own basis for the practice, and where specific communal forms have drawn criticism this is noted and not adjudicated (principles #2 and #7). Category set to 'practice' (this is a practice rather than a doctrine). Relations: part_of sufism, related_to tawhid (the formula of dhikr is often the testimony of God's oneness), related_to ihsan (dhikr cultivates the God-consciousness that ihsan names; ihsan is a sibling entry in this batch). Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern Western academic history (Knysh, Sufism: A New History); (b) two classical primary sources, al-Qushayri's Risala, which treats dhikr among the path's practices, and al-Ghazali's Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din (Book IX, Kitab al-Adhkar wa-l-Da'awat, on invocations and supplications); (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (Nasr, Garden of Truth), whose exposition treats dhikr as the central reality of Sufi life (web-verified). Confidence medium: works verified to exist and be on-topic, relevant passages not read line-by-line. Peer-review fixes (v0.1.0 promotion pass, 2026-07-19): (1) the al-Qushayri source url previously pointed to a Journal of Islamic Studies review of Knysh's translation rather than to the translation itself, and has been replaced with the Garnet Publishing product page; (2) al-Ghazali's Ihya' was previously bundled into the al-Qushayri citation as a 'see also' reference, leaving a source not properly listed. It has been promoted to its own source object (Book IX, on invocations and supplications, web-verified as the relevant book of the Ihya') so no reference in the description rests on an unlisted citation. VERIFIED (2026-07-19): the load-bearing Qur'anic verses (Q 13:28, 33:41) were read directly at quran.com and confirmed as the scriptural basis for the remembrance of God; a Qur'an source is added at high and the entry is promoted to verified. The Sufi-manual sources (Knysh, al-Qushayri, al-Ghazali, Nasr) remain medium (not read line by line).

relations.note: The central devotional discipline of the Sufi path (tasawwuf), individual and communal.
The formula most characteristic of dhikr is the testimony of God's oneness (la ilaha illa Allah), so the practice is bound to tawhid.
Dhikr is described as cultivating the abiding consciousness of God's presence that the term ihsan names.

historical_period.note: Grounded in Qur'anic injunctions to remember God (from 610 CE) and the prophetic example; elaborated as the central Sufi discipline from the formative period and structured into the communal liturgies of the tariqa orders from roughly the 6th-7th/12th-13th centuries.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, Q 13:28 ('in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find comfort', fi dhikri llahi tatma'innu l-qulub) and Q 33:41 ('O believers, remember Allah with much remembrance', udhkuru llaha dhikran kathiran). The primary scriptural basis for dhikr as the remembrance of God.
sources.license: Primary religious text; classical Arabic in the public domain. Verses read directly (2026-07-19) and confirmed.

sources.citation: Knysh, Alexander. Sufism: A New History of Islamic Mysticism. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2017. On dhikr as a defining Sufi practice and its individual and communal forms across the orders.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; not reproduced here.

sources.citation: al-Qushayri, Abu 'l-Qasim. al-Risala al-Qushayriyya fi 'ilm al-tasawwuf (5th/11th c.), trans. Alexander D. Knysh as Al-Qushayri's Epistle on Sufism. Reading: Garnet Publishing, 2007. Classical Sufi manual treating dhikr among the practices and states of the path. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Primary source in scholarly translation; cited for reference.

sources.citation: al-Ghazali, Abu Hamid. Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din (The Revival of the Religious Sciences), late 5th/11th c. Classical primary source; Book IX (Kitab al-Adhkar wa-l-Da'awat, on invocations and supplications) and the sections on the disciplines of the heart treat dhikr among the devotional practices. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Public-domain classical Arabic text; cited for reference.

sources.citation: Nasr, Seyyed Hossein. The Garden of Truth: The Vision and Promise of Sufism, Islam's Mystical Tradition. New York: HarperOne, 2007. A contemporary Muslim scholar's exposition treating dhikr (invocation, remembrance) as the central reality of the life of the Sufis and grounding it in the Qur'anic names of God. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
